posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Me and the boys gathered last night to watch the recording of the Mont Ventoux Tour de France stage, plus knock back some nice wines and pizza. We had an array of wines across the spectrum, but I did want to highlight these two St. Ems.

WOTN for me was the very classic, and so perfectly mature, 1985 La Grange Neuve de Figeac n/k/a Petit Figeac. A beguiling old world perfume right from pop and pour into a decanter. A delicate array of dried red fruits, earth and moist undergrowth, worn saddle leather and tobacco. Medium weight and silky on the palate, probably more on the lighter side even. Just slid so gracefully across the mouth, lovely dried red cherries, tangy plums, more earth and mushrooms, definitely makes you wanna say, sous bois. This wine is perfectly mature, on the back-slope of its existence, tannins and structure fully integrated, modest finish. The wine faded as I circled back a couple hours later, but those first two glasses were so dead-center in my wheelhouse. Just lovely. This bottle eclipsed a recent 2000 that I had. I really liked the 2005 as well, but 2014, the style has changed.

Popping next that 2008 Tertre Roteboeuf, the juxtaposition was funny, you could not find two such polar opposites. And yet somehow, this all worked. Perhaps the pizza melded everything together. This is my second bottle of the 2008 in three months. This is a heady, perfumed, ripe and rich wine. In theory, I should hate it. I don’t. Even the spicy oak does not turn me off. While it definitely throws some dark notes and licorice, it also is balanced with ripe but tangy red fruits, cherries and raspberries. Maybe some Pomerol-like truffle notes. It’s really quite exotic. I do not have a lot of experience with this estate, but did pop a 1999 on Sir William’s recommendation about 6 months ago, and it was a beauty. Probably an excellent vintage for a wine like this, where the vintage perhaps restrains the style. Not sure how a 2015 or 2018 would work for me. Heck, I found the 2015 Ausone recently a bit OTT.

We also enjoyed:

2001 Smith Haut Lafite - major yum
2010 Levet La Chavaroche - Give. It. Time. But, the nose is starting to soar.
2018 Peter Michael Le Pavots - I passed

posts.name: Robert Alfert
Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Nice notes, thanks. Sounds like 2008 TR is ready to drink. I liked the 08 Roc de Cambes well enough, but my one bottle of TR is buried in storage.

Who are you, and what have you done with Alfert?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Haha, maybe Covid really did slay my yak palate!

PS. Notice that I refrained from scoring.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Haha, maybe Covid really did slay my yak palate!

PS. Notice that I refrained from scoring.

Do you ever score wines? I must admit that after reading so many of your tasting notes over the years, I cannot remember whether you score wines or not. Always super clear what you think of a wine, but I have no idea whether you give them numbers. Must be the skills you learned in law school and as a lawyer on how to write precisely.

I hope that you realize that my comments in the above paragraph are about as high a praise as I can give for someone’s tasting notes.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Haha, maybe Covid really did slay my yak palate!

PS. Notice that I refrained from scoring.

Do you ever score wines? I must admit that after reading so many of your tasting notes over the years, I cannot remember whether you score wines or not. Always super clear what you think of a wine, but I have no idea whether you give them numbers. Must be the skills you learned in law school and as a lawyer on how to write precisely.

I hope that you realize that my comments in the above paragraph are about as high a praise as I can give for someone’s tasting notes.

That is awfully kind, Howard, thank you. I normally do score wines as well. I still think in combination with a detailed note it’s a simple way of communicating impressions. I know many people here seem to eschew points, but I’m not there.

posts.name: Marcus Dean
posts.cooked: 1985 produced some crackers for sure, my 2 favourite BDX from the last 5 years have been 85 Palmer and 85 Cheval Blanc.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Haha, maybe Covid really did slay my yak palate!

PS. Notice that I refrained from scoring.

Do you ever score wines? I must admit that after reading so many of your tasting notes over the years, I cannot remember whether you score wines or not. Always super clear what you think of a wine, but I have no idea whether you give them numbers. Must be the skills you learned in law school and as a lawyer on how to write precisely.

I hope that you realize that my comments in the above paragraph are about as high a praise as I can give for someone’s tasting notes.

That is awfully kind, Howard, thank you. I normally do score wines as well. I still think combination with a detailed note it’s a simple way of communicating impressions. I know many people here seem to eschew points, but I’m not there.

I don’t have a problem with points. For wine newsletters I purchase, I want points.

I stopped scoring wines for myself because I really don’t know how to do it in a way that makes sense to me. As you know, my first love is Burgundy. How do I score a Burgundy vs. a Bordeaux? How do I score a German Kabinett vs. an Auslese? I could go on and on but this is only for me. When I go to the Paulee Grand Tasting I admit that I sort of score wines - using stars or something so I can remember my favorites. But, if I tried to score each of the many wines I taste there on a 100 point scale I would spend most of my time trying to figure out whether a new wine is equal to the wine I just rated 90, the one I just rated 90.5, etc., etc., etc., and miss half the wines at the tasting. So, I only do what I can do - probably similar to how I became a tax lawyer.

If you can do it in a way that makes sense to you, between Loire and Bordeaux, say, great, Or if you can do it in a way where points may not make sense by themselves and notes may not make sense by themselves but together they form a synergistic whole, great. Whatever helps you (1) remember the wines you want to remember better and (2) communicate better, you should do. No right or wrong here.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked: I’m always surprised when you comment positively on Tertre Roteboeuf. I love the wine. It’s a great vineyard with a unique way of working in their vineyards and in the cellar.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
1985 produced some crackers for sure, my 2 favourite BDX from the last 5 years have been 85 Palmer and 85 Cheval Blanc.

I had the 85 LLC a few years ago and loved it.

Better than the 1982 and 1986 for me

Having been to way too many LLC verticals, the only ones I have liked are 1985, 1989 and 1996. Never quite understood the love.

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked:
Nice notes Robert…

I must note that, it was the 2001 SHL that got me into Bordeaux, where upon first taste I was thinking to myself, “what on earth is going on here?” Gravel, blackberry slate, earth…yum… As of today, it’s not quite as expressive as it once was in it’s youth but, still showed well with similar characteristics.

As for Bobby’s wine of the night the 1985 La Grange Neuve de Figeac…folks, this is what Bordeaux is all about… Celery salt, moss, leather, damp forest floor… Just had it all, killler juice, what a find!

The '08 Tertre Roteboeuf was a bit polarizing upon opening…before even tasting it, Robert pointed out that due to the large scale this wine would benefit from a decant …We let this sit for about an hour before indulging and it did open up quite nicely… Had a nice exotic sexiness to the palate that I believe was mostly oak driven and a tad heavy, but w/big juicy dark fruits & loads of structure… Okay, I like it but unfortunately, the wine does not inspire me and It’s not something I would ever seek out. Regardless, I can see why others would enjoy it.

The 2010 Levet is quite nice but, not anywhere near peak…w/ a mix of kalamata olive, bacon fat, blackberry and plenty of structure… I’d wait a few years on this…


Les Pavot? No Comment
